session_metadata.title: God (Trinity)
session_metadata.session_topic: Statement of Faith class: doctrine of God as One God eternally existing in three Persons (Trinity), with shared divine attributes and distinct harmonious roles in creation, providence, redemption, and sanctification.

theological_themes: equality of the Persons of the Godhead
distinct and harmonious roles of the Trinity

chunks.content: We believe in One God (Deut 6:4, Mark 12:29). Monotheism is contrasted with other theories such as polytheism.
claims: There is only one God.
The doctrine of one God stands in contrast to polytheism (and to monotheistic alternatives that distort the biblical view).

chunks.content: God created all things (Isa 45:7, Gen 1:1). This creator-claim is contrasted with evolution as an account of origins.
claims: God is the creator of all things.
Biblical creation is presented in contrast to evolutionary accounts of origins.

chunks.content: God sustains all things (Isa 45:7). This is contrasted with Deism, which holds that God created but does not actively uphold creation.
claims: God actively sustains all things.
The doctrine of divine sustenance contradicts Deism's absentee-creator view.

chunks.content: God has eternally existed (Hebrews 9:14, Deut 33:27, Psalm 90:2).
claims: God's existence is eternal — without beginning or end.

chunks.content: God exists eternally in three Persons (Mat 28:19, Matt 3:16, 2 Corinthians 13:14, John 15:26, John 16:3): The Father, The Son (Matthew 28:18), and The Holy Spirit (Acts 5:3-4). The three Persons are equal (Phil 2:6, John 14:6).
themes: equality of the Persons of the Godhead
claims: God is one Being who exists eternally in three distinct Persons: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit.
The Son is fully God (Matthew 28:18 cited for his authority/deity).
The Holy Spirit is fully God (Acts 5:3-4 — lying to the Spirit equated with lying to God).
The three Persons are equal in being and dignity.

chunks.content: Divine attributes — definition: 'Attributes are the perfections which are predicated of the divine being in the scriptures or are visibly exercised by Him in His work of creation, redemption and providence.' (Louis Berkhof)
claims: Divine attributes are the perfections predicated of God in Scripture or displayed by Him in creation, redemption, and providence.

chunks.content: Divine attributes are classified as incommunicable and communicable. Incommunicable perfections are characteristics unique to God (e.g., self-existence, simplicity, immensity). Communicable perfections are characteristics transferable in part to humans (e.g., goodness, righteousness, love).
claims: Divine attributes divide into incommunicable (unique to God) and communicable (partially transferable to humans).
Examples of incommunicable attributes: self-existence, simplicity, immensity.
Examples of communicable attributes: goodness, righteousness, love.

chunks.content: The three Persons are holy in all divine attributes, including: omniscient, holy, just, love, true, free, omnipotent, eternal, infinite, immutable (God is unchanging and unchangeable), omnipresent, and sovereign (1 Peter 1:2).
themes: equality of the Persons of the Godhead
claims: All three Persons of the Godhead share fully in every divine attribute.
God's attributes include omniscience, holiness, justice, love, truth, freedom, omnipotence, eternity, infinity, immutability, omnipresence, and sovereignty.
Immutability means God is unchanging and unchangeable.

chunks.content: The Persons of the Godhead (1 Peter 1:2) perform distinct and harmonious roles in creation, providence, and redemption.
themes: distinct and harmonious roles of the Trinity
claims: Each Person of the Trinity has a distinct role in the divine works of creation, providence, and redemption.
The roles of the three Persons are harmonious — never in conflict.

chunks.content: Creation and preservation are accomplished through the Son (John 1:3, 10; Col 1:16-17; 1 Cor 8:6; Heb 1:2-3, 10) and through the Spirit (Gen 1:2; Job 26:13; 32:8; 33:4; 34:14-15; Ps 104:30).
themes: distinct and harmonious roles of the Trinity
claims: The Son is the agent of creation and preservation.
The Holy Spirit participates in creation and preservation.

chunks.content: Redemption is accomplished through the Father (1 Chron 17:21; Isa 63:16; Gal 4:4-5) and through the Spirit (Heb 9:14; Rom 8:11).
themes: distinct and harmonious roles of the Trinity
claims: The Father is presented as a redeeming agent (sender of the Son in the fullness of time).
The Holy Spirit participates in redemption (e.g., the Spirit by whom Christ offered himself; the Spirit who raises mortal bodies).

chunks.content: Sanctification is accomplished through the Father (John 17:17; 1 Thess 5:23) and through the Son (1 Cor 1:30; Eph 5:25-27).
themes: distinct and harmonious roles of the Trinity
claims: The Father sanctifies his people (e.g., 'Sanctify them by your truth').
The Son sanctifies his people (made unto us sanctification; cleansing the church by the washing of water with the word).

chunks.content: Summary statement: We believe in One God who created and sustains all things. God has eternally existed in three Persons; The Father, The Son, and The Holy Spirit. They are equal and holy in all divine attributes. The Persons of the Godhead perform distinct and harmonious roles in creation, providence, and redemption.
themes: equality of the Persons of the Godhead
distinct and harmonious roles of the Trinity
claims: There is one God who creates and sustains all things.
God has eternally existed in three Persons: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit.
The three Persons are equal and holy in all divine attributes.
The Persons of the Godhead perform distinct yet harmonious roles in creation, providence, and redemption.

embedded_assets.description: Diagram illustrating the Trinity (the classic 'Shield of the Trinity' / Scutum Fidei figure, inferred from context — image content not directly inspected).

raw_extracted_text:
God (Trinity)

We believe
- in One God (Deut 6:4, Mark 12:29) -- Compared to other theories of polytheism, monotheism,
- who created and - Isa 45:7, Gen 1:1 -- Compared to evolution,
- sustains - Isa 45:7 -- compared to Deism
  - all things.
- God has eternally existed - Hebrews 9:14, Deu. 33:27, Psalm 90:2
- in three Persons; (Mat 28:19, Matt. 3:16, 2 Corinthians 13:14, John 15:26, John 16:3
  - The Father,
  - The Son, - Matthew 28:18
  - The Holy Spirit - Acts 5:3-4
  - They are equal -- Phil 2:6, John 14:6,
    - TRINITY
    - [diagram image]
  - holy in all divine attributes.
    - Louis Berkhof: "Attributes are the perfections which are predicated of the divine being in the scriptures or are visibly exercised by Him in His work of creation, redemption and providence"
    - Incommunicable and Communicable
      - The incommunicable perfections are those characteristics unique to God (e.g., self-existence, simplicity, immensity)
      - The communicable perfections are those characteristics transferable in part to humans (e.g., goodness, righteousness, love).
    - omniscient, holy, Just, love, true free, omnipotent, eternal, infinite, immutable (God is unchanging and unchangeable), omnipresent, sovereign,
    - 1 Peter 1:2
- The Persons of the Godhead - 1 Peter 1:2
  - perform distinct and harmonious roles
    - in creation,
    - providence, and
    - redemption.
      - Creation and preservation
        - Through the Son (John 1:3, 10; Col. 1:16-17; 1 Cor. 8:6; Heb. 1:2-3, 10)
        - Through the Spirit (Gen. 1:2; Job 26:13; 32:8; 33:4; 34:14-15; Ps. 104:30)
      - Redemption
        - Through the Father (1 Chron. 17:21; Isa. 63:16; Gal. 4:4-5)
        - Through the Spirit (Heb. 9:14; Rom. 8:11)
      - Sanctification
        - Through the Father (John 17:17; 1 Thess. 5:23)
        - Through the Son (1 Cor. 1:30; Eph. 5:25-27)

We believe in One God who created and sustains all things. God has eternally existed in three Persons; The Father, The Son, and The Holy Spirit. They are equal and holy in all divine attributes. The Persons of the Godhead perform distinct and harmonious roles in creation, providence, and redemption.

Verses: Deut 6:4, Isa 45:7, Mk 12:29, Gen 1:1; Mat 28:19, Mat 3:16, 2 Corinthians 13:14; John 15:26, 16:3, Phil 2, John 14:6; Mat 28:18, Acts 5:3-4, Jn 1:14, Heb 9:13,14, I Pet 1:2
parsing_notes: Document is a Statement of Faith (SOF) class outline on the doctrine of God / Trinity (overview), distinct from the Father-specific SOF document. Format is a bulleted outline with scripture references appended to each proposition; no narrative prose or contributor names appear in the source, so anonymization required no name removal. The Louis Berkhof citation (a published 20th-century systematic theologian) is preserved as a quoted source — he is not a corpus contributor. The source lists 'John 16:3' alongside John 15:26 in the Trinity proof-texts; this is most likely a typo for John 16:13 (Spirit-of-truth context), but recorded as written per quality bar (no hallucinated correction). The closing 'Verses' summary block lists 'Heb 9:13,14' which is normalized as Heb 9:13-14. The classic Trinity diagram (referenced as media/image1.png) was not visually inspected; description is inferred from the 'TRINITY' label adjacent to the image reference. The 'sustenance' bullet was kept in chunk sof_god_03 (matching its outline-level distinction from creation in sof_god_02), and the parallel 'Through the Son / Through the Spirit' structure for creation/redemption/sanctification is preserved across chunks 10-12. Note: the source does NOT explicitly list 'Through the Son' for redemption nor 'Through the Spirit' for sanctification, mirroring an apparent gap in the original outline; the JSON faithfully reflects only what the source records.
